situation: A colleague compliments your Chinese ability

options.feedback: Classic Chinese deflection — humble and culturally perfect.
Acceptable — the 还在学习 adds humility, but a direct 谢谢 is Western-style.

options.text: 是的，我花了很多时间学的。
options.feedback: Accepting compliments directly is uncomfortable in Chinese culture.
过奖 (over-praised) — another classic deflection. Very natural.
Opening gifts immediately is Western — in China, open it later.
Ritual refusal then acceptance is the norm. 太客气了 is perfect.
Warm but misses the expected 推辞 (ritual refusal) step.
破费 (you spent money) — acknowledging their expense is polite.

situation: A friend's parent asks your age
situation_zh: 朋友的父母问你多大了

options.feedback: Direct answer is expected — age isn't private in China.
Refusing to share age seems secretive and rude in Chinese culture.
Playful deflection might work with peers, not with elders.
Answering with zodiac animal is very Chinese — they can calculate your age.
Doubled 不好意思 shows sincerity — the go-to for minor incidents.
对不起 works but implies more guilt — 不好意思 is lighter.
Checking if they're OK is thoughtful but misses the apology.
This tells THEM to be careful — blaming the victim. Don't.

situation: Host keeps offering you more food, you're full
situation_zh: 主人一直让你多吃，你吃饱了

options.feedback: Adding 真的 (really) breaks through the ritual offering cycle.
Doubled 够了 is emphatic. Works, but could sound a bit blunt.

options.text: 我吃不下了，做得太好吃了。
options.feedback: 吃不下 + complimenting the food — peak politeness.
Too blunt — hosts will think you didn't enjoy the food.
